summary:
The Sack of Hao (Haojing, 771 BC) by the Quanrong nomads was one of the pivotal events in Chinese history — the destruction of the Western Zhou capital near modern Xi'an that ended the Western Zhou dynasty and forced the royal court's relocation eastward to Luoyi (modern Luoyang), inaugurating the Eastern Zhou period (Spring and Autumn and Warring States). King You of Zhou was killed in the attack, and the surviving Zhou nobility installed his son Ping as king at the eastern capital.

The political consequences were enormous: the Zhou royal house never recovered its genuine military and political authority. While the Zhou kings retained their ritual and symbolic role as the nominal center of Chinese civilization — the 'Son of Heaven' whose sacrifices maintained cosmic order — actual power devolved to the feudal lords (zhuhou) of the many Zhou states. The [[event:1953|Zhou Dynasty Spring Autumn]] period that followed was one of sophisticated interstate diplomacy and cultural achievement, but of fundamental Zhou royal weakness. The sack and relocation is thus the Chinese equivalent of Rome's sack by the Gauls (390 BC) in its symbolic impact — a traumatic rupture that ended a golden age and began a new, more complex era of competing powers.
